statement: Gallup polls conducted in Iceland between 2022 and early 2026 show a gradual shift in public opinion on EU membership. Support for applying for EU membership rose from approximately 30% in 2022 to around 45% by late 2025, while opposition declined from roughly 50% to 40%. The remainder were undecided. The trend accelerated following the Althingi's decision in 2024 to hold a binding referendum.
caveats: Polling methodology and question wording vary between surveys. Some polls ask about 'applying for membership' while others ask about 'joining the EU', which can produce different results. Undecided voters remain a large and potentially decisive group.

statement: Polling data consistently shows that support for EU membership in Iceland is higher among younger voters (18–34: ~55% in favour), urban residents in the capital region (~50%), and those with university education (~52%). Opposition is strongest among older voters (55+: ~50% opposed), rural residents (~55% opposed), and those employed in agriculture and fisheries (~65% opposed).
source_name: Félagsvísindastofnun Háskóla Íslands — Social Science Research Institute
caveats: Demographic breakdowns in Icelandic polls have smaller sample sizes per subgroup (total population ~390,000), increasing margin of error for individual categories. Cross-tabulations should be interpreted with caution.

statement: When asked to rank the most important issues in the EU debate, Icelandic voters consistently prioritise fisheries policy (cited by ~60% as a top concern), sovereignty and democratic self-governance (~50%), economic stability and currency (~45%), and agricultural policy (~30%). Environmental and climate policy has risen from ~15% in 2022 to ~25% in 2025 as a salient issue.
caveats: Issue salience is sensitive to framing and current events. Rankings may shift significantly in the months leading up to the referendum as campaign messaging intensifies.

statement: Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) and Viðreisn (Reform Party) are the two Althingi parties that formally support EU membership. Samfylkingin has supported EU membership since 2007 and led the 2009 application process while in government. Viðreisn was founded in 2016 partly on a pro-EU platform and has consistently advocated for completing accession negotiations. Together they held approximately 17 of 63 Althingi seats after the 2024 elections.
source_name: Althingi — Party platforms and election results
caveats: Party positions can evolve. Individual MPs may diverge from the official party line. Seat counts reflect the most recent election and may change with by-elections or party defections.

statement: Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party), Framsóknarflokkurinn (Progressive Party), Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) oppose EU membership. The Independence Party has historically been the strongest opponent, citing sovereignty and fisheries concerns. The Progressive Party, traditionally representing rural interests, opposes membership primarily over agricultural and fisheries policy. These four parties collectively held approximately 33–35 of 63 Althingi seats after the 2024 elections.
source_name: Althingi — Party platforms and election results
caveats: The Independence Party has factions with varying views on EU engagement. Some members support the EEA but oppose full membership. Framsóknarflokkurinn's position has softened slightly in recent years, with some members open to revisiting the question.

statement: Vinstrihreyfingin – grænt framboð (Left-Green Movement) and Píratar (Pirate Party) do not have unified positions on EU membership. The Left-Greens are internally divided: some members support membership for environmental and social policy alignment, while others oppose it on sovereignty and democratic grounds. The party's official position favours a referendum but does not take a side. Píratar support direct democracy and have backed the referendum process without endorsing a specific outcome, though individual MPs lean both ways.
caveats: Internal party dynamics are fluid. Both parties may adopt clearer positions as the referendum approaches. Their voters' preferences do not necessarily track party leadership statements.

statement: Samtök atvinnulífsins (SA, Confederation of Icelandic Enterprise) has broadly supported EU membership, arguing it would reduce trade barriers, lower the cost of capital, and provide a more stable regulatory environment. Alþýðusamband Íslands (ASÍ, Icelandic Confederation of Labour) has been more cautious, supporting the referendum process but not formally endorsing membership. ASÍ's concerns centre on whether EU labour market rules would weaken the Icelandic collective bargaining model, which achieves high union density (~90%) and strong wage outcomes.
source_name: SA and ASÍ public statements and policy documents
caveats: SA and ASÍ are umbrella organisations whose member federations may hold divergent views. Formal position papers may differ from the views expressed by individual leaders in media.

statement: Landssamband íslenskra útvegsmanna (LÍÚ, Federation of Icelandic Fishing Vessel Owners) and Samtök fyrirtækja í sjávarútvegi (SFS, Iceland Ocean Cluster / Fisheries Association) strongly oppose EU membership. Their primary concern is that Iceland's individual transferable quota (ITQ) fisheries management system and exclusive control over the 200-nautical-mile exclusive economic zone would be subordinated to the EU's Common Fisheries Policy (CFP). The fishing industry contributes approximately 8% of GDP directly and ~25% when upstream and downstream activities are included.
source_name: LÍÚ and SFS public statements; Hagstofa Íslands national accounts
caveats: The fisheries sector's economic weight has declined relative to tourism and technology in recent decades. The sector's political influence may exceed its current GDP share due to historical importance and regional employment concentration. Any accession negotiation would likely involve special arrangements for fisheries, as was discussed during the 2010–2013 negotiations.
